turns.user_message: I dropped a glass of water on the kitchen floor yesterday and just stood there staring at the mess. It felt like such a stupid little thing, but I couldn't stop apologizing to the empty room. I know it was just an accident, yet I spent the whole afternoon replaying it in my head. I keep wondering why I can't just brush off minor mistakes like everyone else seems to. It’s only been a few days since this started, but it already feels exhausting.

That same clumsy feeling keeps creeping back into my daily routine now. Every time I misplace my keys or send a slightly awkward text, I immediately assume I've ruined everything. I find myself walking on eggshells around my own shadow, waiting for the next thing to go wrong. It’s like my brain has decided to treat every tiny slip-up as a major character flaw. I just want to stop overthinking these little moments before they pile up into something bigger.

I am completely convinced that I am fundamentally broken beyond repair because of how I handle these everyday stumbles. Every single misstep feels like undeniable proof that I am just a burden to anyone who has to deal with me. I catch myself holding my breath before opening a door or picking up a pen, terrified of making another mess. The weight of all these imagined failures is crushing me from the inside out. I don't know how to convince myself that I am allowed to exist without constantly apologizing for taking up space.

generation_metadata.diversity_anchor: The speaker opens by referencing a specific recent moment. This just started recently.
